coredata.dc:title: Implementation of high-performance flexible electrochromic device based on polyaniline/ carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Ting
Gao, Xiaohui
Zhang, Pin
Qin, Longlong
Li, Chen
Xie, Haipeng
Huang, Shengxiang
Liao, Congwei
Deng, Lianwen
coredata.dc:description: The electrochromic (EC) process is essentially an electrochemical reaction associated with the intercalation (de-intercalation) of ions to (from) the EC film. To improve the infrared emissivity modulation performances of the infrared electrochromic devices (IR-ECDs), polyaniline/carbon quantum dots (PANI/CDs) flexible films were fabricated by the electropolymerized approach in this paper. The prepared PANI/CDs porous films were exhibited and verified excellent electrochromic properties through micromorphology analysis and electrochemical tests. The measured emittance variation (Δε) of the prepared PANI/CDs samples are 0.41, 0.46, and 0.42 in ranges of wavelength 3–5 μm, 8–14 μm, and 2.5–25 μm, respectively. In addition, for the infrared range of 2.5–25 μm, the radiation energy can be continuously modulated from 80.77 to 241.98 W/m2 (ΔP = 161.21 W/m2). The assembled IR-ECD based on PANI/CDs films obtains excellent IR modulation ability, that the emittance variations are 0.39, 0.41, and 0.37 in the wavelength ranges of 3–5 μm, 8–14 μm, and 2.5–25 μm, respectively. The switching times of the device for the coloration and bleaching are 3.1 s and 3.7 s respectively, much shorter than that of previously reported ECDs based on PANI. Meanwhile, the developed ECD presents decent durability, as the Δε values of IR-ECD in wavelength ranges of 3–5 μm, 8–14 μm, and 2.5–25 μm maintaining radio of 75%, 76%, and 68%, and current maintaining ratio of the device is above 90% after aging measurements of consecutive 100 cycles. Furthermore, the integrated IR-ECD was proposed to achieve dynamic conversion from golden yellow to dark green in the visible light regime through precise tuning of external biasing voltage. The proposed ECD based on PANI/CDs technology is promising for the future of adaptive IR sensors and thermoregulation-related applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochromic device
Carbon quantum dots
Polyaniline
Infrared emissivity
Switching speed

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the PANI/CDs porous film
Fabrication of the infrared electrochromic device

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of surface on (a) PANI/CDs and (b) PANI porous films. The cross section of (c) PANI/CDs and (d) PANI porous films.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, the inset gives TEM images of the CDs. (b) XRD patterns of CDs, PANI/CDs film and PANI film. High-resolution TEM images of (c) prepared PANI/CDs film, and (d) lattice spacing of CDs.

(a) CV curves of PANI/CDs and PANI porous films measured in 0.5 M H2SO4 aqueous solutions with a scan rate of 50 mV/s sweeping between −0.3 V and 1.2 V. (b) Electrochemical impedance spectra and corresponding simulation results (fitting lines) of PANI/CDs and PANI films, the inset shows Nyquist plots of the films in the high-frequency region, and equivalent Randles circuit model.
Emittance curves (a, b) and emissive power curves (c, d) of PANI/CDs and PANI porous films at −0.25 V and 0.55 V.

(a)The emissivity value of PANI/CDs IR-ECD at the spectrum (3–5 μm and 8–14 μm), the inset gives digital camera images of PANI/CDs IR-ECD at −0.8 V and 0.8 V. (b) The transient current response for PANI/CDs IR-ECD under −0.8 V and 0.8 V. Measured Δε (c) and current stability (d) of the PANI/CDs IR-ECD during 100 redox cycles.

Schematic of the (a) cross-section and (b) top view of integrated IR-ECD based on PANI/CDs. The IR-ECD based on PANI/CDs under the left electrode (c) or the right electrode (d) is separately biased at different voltages.
(a) Conceptual illustration of the proposed fabrication process, and device structure for the IR-ECD based on PANI/CDs.

Δε
a and ΔP
b data of the prepared porous films in different wavebands.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Δε = ε 0.55V - ε −0.25V. The value of emittance varies in different states.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: ΔP = P 0.55V – P −0.25V. The value of emissive power in different states.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of IR-ECDs parameters for PANI with literature.

head.ce:title: Implementation of high-performance flexible electrochromic device based on polyaniline/ carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Electronics, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Electronics, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Key Laboratory on Electromagnetic Environmental Effects and Electro-optical Engineering, Army Engineering University of PLA, Nanjing, 210007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Key Laboratory on Electromagnetic Environmental Effects and Electro-optical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Key Laboratory on Electromagnetic Environmental Effects and Electro-optical Engineering, Army Engineering University of PLA, Nanjing 210007, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The electrochromic (EC) process is essentially an electrochemical reaction associated with the intercalation (de-intercalation) of ions to (from) the EC film. To improve the infrared emissivity modulation performances of the infrared electrochromic devices (IR-ECDs), polyaniline/carbon quantum dots (PANI/CDs) flexible films were fabricated by the electropolymerized approach in this paper. The prepared PANI/CDs porous films were exhibited and verified excellent electrochromic properties through micromorphology analysis and electrochemical tests. The measured emittance variation (Δε) of the prepared PANI/CDs samples are 0.41, 0.46, and 0.42 in ranges of wavelength 3–5 μm, 8–14 μm, and 2.5–25 μm, respectively. In addition, for the infrared range of 2.5–25 μm, the radiation energy can be continuously modulated from 80.77 to 241.98 W/m2 (ΔP = 161.21 W/m2). The assembled IR-ECD based on PANI/CDs films obtains excellent IR modulation ability, that the emittance variations are 0.39, 0.41, and 0.37 in the wavelength ranges of 3–5 μm, 8–14 μm, and 2.5–25 μm, respectively. The switching times of the device for the coloration and bleaching are 3.1 s and 3.7 s respectively, much shorter than that of previously reported ECDs based on PANI. Meanwhile, the developed ECD presents decent durability, as the Δε values of IR-ECD in wavelength ranges of 3–5 μm, 8–14 μm, and 2.5–25 μm maintaining radio of 75%, 76%, and 68%, and current maintaining ratio of the device is above 90% after aging measurements of consecutive 100 cycles. Furthermore, the integrated IR-ECD was proposed to achieve dynamic conversion from golden yellow to dark green in the visible light regime through precise tuning of external biasing voltage. The proposed ECD based on PANI/CDs technology is promising for the future of adaptive IR sensors and thermoregulation-related applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochromic devices (ECDs) have been widely applied for smart windows, infrared (IR) sensing, and optical switching in visible and infrared light ranges [1–3]. Recently, conducting polymer (CP) based on polyaniline (PANI) become a research focus owing to its high optical contrast, wide band tuneability, low power consumption, and fast switching speed [4–9], compared with the inorganic electrochromic material [10,11]. Especially, PANI based ECDs have been implemented for IR applications for their excellent IR emissivity (ε) modulation ability [12–20]. For example, P. Chandrasekhar et al. assembled the reflective ECDs based on the poly (anethosulfonate)-doped PANI films, the IR emittance of which varied from 0.32 to 0.79 in ranges of wavelength 0–40 μm [13]. Li et al. prepared the ECDs based on H2SO4-doped PANI films as the EC layer, the devices showed emittance variation (Δε) of 0.24 in ranges of wavelength 8–12 μm [14]. Moreover, Li's group has carried out IR ECDs using different acid-doped PANI films, achieving the Δε of 0.4 within the wavelength range of 2.5–25 μm [15–17,19,20]. However, some limitations of acid-doped PANI materials need to be further investigated, namely the IR electrochromic controllability, and the slow response.

Synthesis of composites by PANI with other nanomaterials is an effective strategy for improving the electrochromic properties. Compared with the insulated nanomaterials, the highly conductive metal nanomaterials [21,22] and carbon nanomaterials demonstrate enhanced electrochromic properties of the PANI [23–28]. For PANI film, the change of color is due to the transition between different oxidation states accompanying the intercalation (de-intercalation) of ions. Thus, larger ion conductivity and surface areas of the film can achieve adequate redox reaction, which can improve the optical contrast [29]. As a new class of carbonaceous material, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have attracted more attention in electronic applications for their good electrical conductivity, large surface area, and excellent chemical stability [30–32]. It has been reported that the PANI/CDs composites show better electrochemical performance [33–35]. However, up to date, there are few reports on CDs combined with PANI materials for IR electrochromic applications.

Herein, the high electrochromic performance IR-ECD based on PANI/CDs porous films was prepared to employ the electropolymerized method on the Au/PES porous substrate. The electrochromic properties of the PANI/CDs porous films were investigated as the IR-ECD functional layer by micromorphology analysis and electrochemical tests. Finally, the IR-ECD based on PANI/CDs integration method will be demonstrated and explored, which features voltage programming ability with a reduced external driving signal line.

Aniline (99.5%, AR) was from Aladdin Company and distilled under reduced pressure. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4), citric acid (CA), and ethanediamine were from the Alfa-Aesar Company. Poly (methyl methacrylate) PMMA, lithium perchlorate (LiClO4, ≥95%), acetonitrile (ACN, ≥99.8%), and propylene carbonate (PC, ≥99.8%) were from Aladdin chemistry. Microporous substrates of polyethersulfone (PES) and polyethylene (PE) films were from commercial suppliers. The Au layer was deposited on the microporous PES substrate by thermal evaporation with a thickness of 200 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the PANI/CDs porous film
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method according to the literature [36]. Specifically, 0.42 g of CA and 0.5 mL of ethanediamine, were dissolved into 10 mL of water with ultrasonic mixing for 10 min. The homogeneous solution was transferred into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (30 mL). After being heated at 200 °C for 4 h, it had been dialyzed against water for 1 day, with the orange liquid as CDs solution. Synthesis of the PANI/CDs films proceeded through the electropolymerization on the Au/PES microporous membrane (1 × 1 cm2) in the 30 mL of the aqueous solution of 1 M H2SO4, 0.5 M aniline monomer, and 150 μL (6 mg) CDs solution. The chronoamperometry method used the potential of 0.7 V, and different polymerization charges of 0.5C/cm2, respectively. The Ag/AgCl and Au/PES microporous membranes (2 × 2 cm2) were used as the reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. The electrochemical polymerization curves were shown in Scheme 1(c). After the electropolymerization, the prepared films, marked as PANI/CDs, were washed with ultrapure water, and then air-dried at the temperature of 40 °C. Meanwhile, a parallel experiment was prepared by the same procedure as PANI/CDs without the addition of CDs, marked as PANI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the infrared electrochromic device
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication route of the IR electrochromic device was displayed in Scheme 1 . The gel electrolyte was prepared according to the report [37]. Specifically, LiClO4 was dissolved into a mixture of 20 mL PC and 70 mL ACN. Afterward, 7 g PMMA was added little by little to the solution and the mixture was stirred overnight. Finally, a transparent gel electrolyte was obtained. After depositing PANI/CDs on the Au/PES by electrochemical polymerization, a gel electrolyte was cast on the surface of PANI/CDs film (counter electrode), and another PANI/CDs as the front electrode was coated on the gel electrolyte layer. Finally, the IR electrochromic device was assembled after hot packaging with two pieces of PE.

The morphology of the prepared films was studied using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM S4800, Hitachi, Japan). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization was implemented using the Tecnai G2 F20. The surface morphology and rough degree of the prepared film were examined with an atomic force microscope (AFM 5500, Agilent Technologies). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were conducted on a Rigaku D Max 2500 Powder Diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiations (λ = 0.15406 nm). The conductivities of films were measured by the standard four-probe method at room temperature with pellet samples (Four-point Probe Meter, SDY-4D, China). Electrochemical measurements of PANI/CDs and PANI porous film were performed in a three-electrode model with 0.5 M H2SO4 aqueous solutions as the electrolyte. Graphite rod and Ag/AgCl were utilized as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. For the characterization of ECDs, the electrochemical tests were carried out using the two-electrode model. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and chronoamperometric (CA) experiments were done by an electrochemical workstation (CHI760E, Shanghai Chenhua Instruments, China). EIS was conducted with 1 mV perturbation amplitude over the frequency range of 0.01 Hz–1 MHz. IR reflectance and absorptance spectra (FTIR) of the PANI/CDs and PANI films at different voltages were carried out by Nicolet IS50 Fourier IR spectroscopy (America).

The surface morphology and profiles of prepared films were investigated by SEM images as shown in Fig. 1 . The PANI and PANI/CDs porous films present a porous structure, which is beneficial to electrolyte infiltration and ion transmission [38]. As shown in Fig. 1(c and d), all the layers with a thickness of 30 μm are tightly attached to the substrate membrane, presenting uniform micropore structures. Figs. S3(a and b) depict 2D and 3D images of PANI/CDs and PANI films in an area of 5 × 5 μm2 by AFM. The roughness Ra values of PANI and PANI/CDs films are measured to be 56.9 nm, and 86.5 nm, respectively. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. S3 (c), the porosity of the PANI/CDs films is four times that of the PANI films. Herein, the surface of the PANI/CDs film is rougher and more porous compared with PANI film, which facilitates a rapid electrochemical reaction of films. It may be caused that the doped carbon component is beneficial to increasing the polymerization reaction rate (Fig. S2(b)), and grows perpendicular to the polymer backbone chain, building a more reticular structure and rough membrane.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs was taken in Fig. 1(a). The peak around 220 nm is assigned to π- π* transitions, and that at 350 nm is ascribed to n-π* transitions [39–41]. Meanwhile, the inset TEM images reveal the structure of CDs with small dots in the diameter range of 5 nm–10 nm.

The XRD pattern of CDs exhibits a broad peak of about 25° (Fig. 2 (b)), which shows a diffraction peak corresponding to 3.4 Å, corresponding to the (002) facet of graphite [36]. As reported, the different diffraction peak of PANI usually relates to the mono-distribution of the periodicity between the polymer backbone chains [42]. Specifically, the broad peak at 2θ = 20° and 23° are assigned to the periodicity parallel to the polymer chains of PANI, and periodicity perpendicular to the polymer backbone chain, respectively [43]. Compared to the pure PANI, the peak of PANI/CDs at 25° becomes stronger, suggesting that the molecular arrangement of PANI in PANI/CDs composite becomes ordered by the introduction of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 (c, d), the high-resolution TEM image confirmed the presence of CDs in the PANI/CDs films. The micrograph shows the regular lattice spacing of 0.338 nm for CDs (Fig. 1(d)), in accordance with the XRD results.

The electrochemical properties of the films were investigated by cyclic voltammetry in the 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with the sweeping voltage potential of −0.3 V and 1.2 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at the scan rate of 50 mV/s. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the PANI/CDs film exhibits two pairs of redox peaks, corresponding to the transformation from leucoemeraldine base (LB) to emeraldine salt (ES), and ES to pernigraniline base (PNB) reversible transitions [44,45]. The redox reaction is as follows (1):
(1) PANI/CDs•SO4 2− + e− +2H+ (Oxidized state) ⇔ PANI/CDs0•SO4 2−+2H+ (Reduced state)
where e− and H+ indicate the electron and proton of the H2SO4 electrolyte, respectively.

The redox peaks currents of the PANI/CDs film are much higher than those of the PANI film, for the PANI/CDs composite presents better electrochemical activities because of the incorporation of CDs. According to the XRD results, ordered PANI chains are beneficial to inter-chain charge transfer. Therefore, the PANI/CDs film should exhibit larger electrical conductivities as compared with the PANI film. As expected, the conductivity of PANI/CDs film (1.67 S/cm) is much larger than that of PANI film (0.24 S/cm). The position of the oxidation peak shifts to 0.45 V, and that of the reduction peak shifts to 0.74 V for the PANI/CDs film, which indicates lower charge-transfer resistance of PANI/CDs film. Furthermore, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy also demonstrates this result (Fig. 3(b)). The Nyquist plots were fitted by an equivalent Randles circuit model (inset Fig. 2b). As shown in Fig. 3(b), the charge-transfer resistance (R
CT ) values of PANI/CDs and PANI films are 4.67 Ω and 17.33 Ω, respectively. The decrease in R
CT can be attributed to the increase of electric conductivity and ionic conductivity of PANI/CDs by introducing the CDs. In addition, the integral area based on the CV area of the PANI/CDs film is much larger than that of the PANI film, indicating that the PANI/CDs film possess of better ion storage capacity [29].

The emittance curves of prepared films were measured to investigate the IR modulation at potentials of −0.25 V and 0.55 V as shown in Fig. 4. The relevant values of emittance variation (Δε) in 3–5 μm, 8–14 μm, and 2.5–25 μm were calculated according to Eq. (1-3) (Supplementary information) to quantitatively evaluate the IR regulation, and the results were shown in Table 1. The PANI/CDs film realizes modulation of optimum emittance variation from 0.48 to 0.90 (Δε = 0.42) in the wavelength range of 2.5–25 μm, 0.24 to 0.65 (Δε = 0.41) in 3–5 μm, and 0.22 to 0.68 (Δε = 0.46) in 8–14 μm, respectively. Meanwhile, the emissive power of PANI/CDs films can realize modulation of radiation energy variation from 80.77 to 241.98 W/m2 (ΔP = 161.21 W/m2) in 2.5–25 μm, which is superior to that of PANI films (ΔP = 85.56 W/m2). Obtaining larger IR emissivity modulation of PANI depends on regulating effective transformation between the various states of PANI [19]. Therefore, PANI/CDs films present enhanced IR electrochromic performance. It can be attributed to the more rough and porous structure possessed by PANI/CDs film, which can provide more accessible reaction sites, leading to more PANI units being effectively switched. Because of these reversible transformations of PANI/CDs film between the ES and LE states, accurate electrical IR modulation could be achieved by the PANI/CDs based devices.

The switching time and durability of ECD are key parameters for practical applications. CA tests of PANI/CDs IR-ECD with voltage steps between −0.8 V (15 s) and 0.8 V (15 s) were conducted to investigate the switching time of IR-ECD as shown in Fig. 5 (b). The switching times of PANI/CDs IR-ECD for the coloration and bleaching are 3.1 s and 3.7 s, respectively, which are shorter than those of the reported results [46,47] (Table 2). The improvement in switching speeds is owing to the rough and porous structure, and smaller interface resistance between the surface and electrolyte of PANI/CDs films.

The durability of PANI/CDs IR-ECD was evaluated by emittance modulation and CA measurements. Fig. 5(c) displays the Δε values of IR-ECD in wavelength ranges of 3–5 μm, 8–14 μm, and 2.5–25 μm maintaining 75%, 76%, and 68% after 100 cycles, respectively. Meanwhile, the current cyclability of PANI/CDs IR-ECD during a 100-cycle test is shown in Fig. 5(d). It maintains about 90% of the highest value after 100 cycles, much more stable than pure PANI IR-ECD [15] (Table 2). The improved stability of the PANI IR-ECD could be attributed to more ordered chains of PANI/CDs film, which is more stable than disorderly chains of PANI in the electrochromic reactions [29].

To reduce the additional external biasing lines and controlling signals of the external circuit board, an integrated IR-ECD based on PANI/CDs structure and fabrication approach were proposed as shown in Fig. 6 . It illustrates the schematic cross-section and top view of the integrated IR-ECD based on PANI/CDs, respectively. The integrated IR-ECD was composed of 5 components, namely upper/lower PE protection, front/counter electrode, and electrolyte layer, respectively. Particularly, the two pieces of the front electrode were assembled on the one counter electrode (PANI/CDs) which is beneficial for the wide-scale application in IR stealth. The optical images of the integrated IR-ECD in the colored and bleached state are shown in Fig. 6(c) and (d). The ECD_L and ECD_R achieved separate bias at different voltages under three signal lines, and transformed from golden yellow to dark green upon the external voltage of −0.8 V and 0.8 V. As shown in Fig. S4 (Supplementary Information), the number of signal lines is reduced to 3/2*n2 by taking an integrated IR-ECD as a sub pixel unit in n*n pixel array, while there are 2*n2 of signal lines with the single IR-ECD.

To sum up, the high-performance electrochromic device based on polyaniline/carbon quantum dots flexible films was proposed and prepared via the electropolymerization process on the Au/porous substrates. The fabricated IR-ECD based on PANI/CDs presents Δε of 0.39, 0.41, and 0.37 in wavelength ranges of 3–5 μm, 8–14 μm, and 2.5–25 μm, respectively. The measured response times are 3.1 s and 3.7 s for the coloration and bleaching processes, respectively. The prepared electrochromic layer of IR-ECD exhibits enhanced electrochemical performance compared with the state-of-the-art counterparts. Simultaneously, the ECD shows strong IR modulation ability and rapid response time. Furthermore, the excellent IR electrochromic properties of the integrated IR-ECD pave a promising path for adaptive IR sensors and thermal management systems.

Ting Qin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing-original draft. Xiaohui Gao: Conceptualization, Methodology. Pin Zhang: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Longlong Qin: Data curation, Methodology. Chen Li: Methodology, Data curation. Haipeng Xie: Supervision, Methodology. Shengxiang Huang: Supervision, Methodology. Congwei Liao: Conceptualization, Writing-review & editing. Lianwen Deng: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing-review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2017YFA0204600), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21902186 and 21905305), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of Central South University (Grant No. 2020zzts052).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.orgel.2022.106716.
